chunk_summary: Guidelines for formula volume prescribing and breastfeeding-protective mixed feeding emphasize weight-based formula intake, paced feeding, and strategies to protect breastfeeding, including reassessment and gradual reduction of supplements.
learning_objectives: Understand weight-based formula volume prescribing and its role in preventing underfeeding or overfeeding.
Learn strategies to protect breastfeeding during mixed feeding, including breastfeeding-first workflows and paced bottle feeding.
Recognize the importance of reassessment timelines and gradual reduction of formula supplements.
Identify risks of excessive milk volumes displacing iron-rich complementary foods after 6 months.
key_facts: Total milk intake in early infancy is approximately 150 mL/kg/day, with most infants not requiring more than 960 mL/day.
Paced bottle feeding involves a horizontal bottle position, frequent pauses, and stopping at satiety cues.
In mixed feeding, breastfeeding should occur first, with formula supplementing only the remaining gap.
Reassess newborns within 24–48 hours and older stable infants within 1 week to adjust feeding plans.
After 6 months, avoid large milk volumes to prevent displacement of iron-rich complementary foods.
reference_quote: A practical anchor in early infancy is around 150 mL/kg/day total milk intake (from formula plus breast milk), recognizing normal variation; many infants do not need more than about 960 mL/day.
document_key_topics: Reassessment and gradual reduction of formula supplements
Avoiding displacement of complementary foods after 6 months
